title: What Is a Budget?
nceaFocus: AS92028 — describing options, using a budget as a financial tool
objectives: Define income and expenses in a NZ context
Explain the 50/30/20 rule with real examples
Understand what pūtake means in a financial context

vocabulary.definition: A plan that shows how much money you earn and how you will spend or save it. A budget is a financial tool, not a restriction.
Money coming IN — wages, allowances, benefits, rental income. Gross income is before tax. Net income is what actually hits your bank account.
Money going OUT — rent, food, transport, entertainment, subscriptions. Fixed expenses stay the same each month. Variable expenses change.
When your income is greater than your expenses. You have money left over. This is what you want.
When your expenses are greater than your income. You are spending more than you earn. This leads to debt if it continues.

In Commerce, pūtake is the reason an organisation exists — its purpose and values. For example, a food bank's pūtake is to ensure no one in their community goes hungry. Pūtake shapes every financial decision.

activities.title: Why Most Kiwis Struggle With Money
activities.content:
Here's something nobody tells you at school: most financial stress doesn't come from not earning enough money. It comes from having no plan for the money you do earn.

A 2024 survey found that 47% of New Zealanders couldn't cover an unexpected $1,000 expense without going into debt or selling something. That's nearly half of all Kiwis — including plenty who earn good salaries.

The single most powerful financial habit you can build right now isn't earning more. It's knowing exactly where your money is going.

That's what a budget does. It's not about being tight with money. It's about being intentional with it. When you have a plan, every dollar has a job — and you stop wondering where your money went at the end of the week.

Today you're going to learn the most important financial tool you'll ever use. And unlike most things you learn at school, you'll use this literally every week for the rest of your life.

questions.question: What is the most common reason Kiwis struggle financially?
options: They have no plan for the money they earn
The cost of living is too high
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that most financial stress comes from a lack of planning, not a lack of income. A budget gives every dollar a purpose.

questions.question: What is a budget?
options: A limit on how much you can spend
A plan for how you earn and use your money
questions.explanation: A budget is a plan — not a restriction. It shows your income, your planned expenses, and how much you'll save.

activities.title: Income vs Expenses — The Fundamental Equation
activities.content:
Every financial situation in the world comes down to one equation:

  INCOME − EXPENSES = SURPLUS (good) or DEFICIT (bad)

Income is money coming IN. In New Zealand, common income sources for students include:
• Part-time wages (minimum wage is $23.15/hr in 2025)
• Allowances from whānau
• Government payments like the Student Allowance
• Side income from selling things online or doing odd jobs

Expenses are money going OUT. These fall into categories:
• FIXED: the same every month (phone plan, bus pass, subscriptions)
• VARIABLE: changes month to month (groceries, petrol, entertainment)
• IRREGULAR: comes up occasionally (car rego, dentist, school trips)

Here's a real example. Aroha works 10 hours per week at a café earning $23.15/hr. Her gross weekly income is $231.50. After PAYE tax at 10.5%, her net income is about $207.29.

Her weekly expenses: phone $12, bus pass $20, lunch $25, streaming services $5 = $62 total.

Surplus: $207.29 − $62 = $145.29 per week she could save.

But Aroha doesn't save any of it. By Friday she's spent it all and doesn't know where it went. Does that sound familiar?

activities.title: Key Terms — Financial Foundations
activities.instruction: Read each term and its definition. These are the building blocks of everything in this module.

questions.question: Tama earns $320 per week and spends $415 per week. What is his financial situation?
questions.explanation: $320 − $415 = −$95. Tama is spending $95 more than he earns each week. This is a deficit. If this continues, he will accumulate debt.

questions.question: Which of the following is a FIXED expense?
questions.explanation: A fixed expense is the same amount every month. A phone plan is a contract with a set monthly fee. Groceries and petrol vary — they are variable expenses.

questions.question: Aroha earns $207 per week net and spends $62 on essentials. How much could she save weekly?
questions.explanation: $207 − $62 = $145. This is her potential weekly surplus if she doesn't spend on anything else. Over a year, that's $7,540 she could save.

activities.title: The 50/30/20 Rule — Your First Budget Framework
activities.content:
The 50/30/20 rule is the simplest budgeting framework in the world, and it works whether you earn $200 or $2,000 a week.

50% NEEDS — things you genuinely cannot do without:
• Rent/board contribution
• Food and groceries
• Transport to work or school
• Essential phone plan
• Healthcare

30% WANTS — things that improve your life but aren't essential:
• Eating out and takeaways
• Entertainment (Netflix, Spotify, games)
• Clothing beyond the basics
• Hobbies

20% SAVINGS — treat this as non-negotiable:
• Emergency fund (goal: 3 months of expenses)
• KiwiSaver contributions
• Saving for a specific goal (car, travel, deposit)

Let's apply it to a real NZ example:

Liam earns $400/fortnight from his part-time job at Pak'nSave.
• 50% ($200) → needs: bus pass $40, food $80, phone $45, board $35
• 30% ($120) → wants: Uber Eats $40, PS Plus $20, clothing $60
• 20% ($80) → savings: $80 straight to savings account the day he gets paid

The most important trick: set up an automatic transfer to savings the moment your pay lands. Pay yourself first — before you can spend it.

activities.title: The Corbin Challenge
activities.setup:
Corbin is 17 and lives at home with his whānau in Hamilton. He works 15 hours per week at The Warehouse earning $23.15/hr. His monthly NET income (after tax) is approximately $1,200.

His current monthly expenses:
• Phone: $45
• Bus pass: $80
• Lunch at school: $120 (buys every day)
• Spotify + Netflix: $30
• Clothing: $100
• Entertainment (movies, nights out): $150
• Savings: $0

Total expenses: $525
Surplus: $675
activities.challenge:
1. Calculate Corbin's monthly surplus (done above — $675). Where do you think this $675 is actually going?

2. Apply the 50/30/20 rule to Corbin's income. What should he be spending in each category?

3. Identify two expenses you would reduce and explain why, considering both the financial impact and Corbin's wellbeing.

4. Corbin wants to save for a car worth $8,000. Using the 20% savings rule, how many months will it take him to reach his goal?
hints: To find months to goal: divide $8,000 by monthly savings amount
activities.solution:
50/30/20 applied: Needs $600, Wants $360, Savings $240.

Corbin's needs total $245 — well under $600. His wants total $280 — under his $360 wants budget. But he's saving $0.

At $240/month savings, Corbin reaches $8,000 in approximately 33 months (just under 3 years).

If he cut his lunch spend from $120 to $40 (by bringing lunch 3 days per week) and reduced entertainment to $80, he could save an extra $150/month — reaching his goal in about 21 months instead.

questions.question: According to the 50/30/20 rule, what percentage of income should go to savings?
questions.explanation: 20% is the savings allocation in the 50/30/20 rule. This includes KiwiSaver contributions, emergency funds, and saving for goals.

questions.question: Which of these is a WANT, not a NEED?
questions.explanation: Streaming services (Netflix, Spotify) are wants — they improve quality of life but are not essential for survival or participation in work/school. Transport, a basic phone, and food are needs.

questions.question: Mia earns $1,500/month and applies the 50/30/20 rule. How much should she save per month?
questions.explanation: 20% of $1,500 = $300. Mia should automatically transfer $300 to savings on payday before spending anything else.

questions.question: What does pūtake mean in a Commerce context?
options: The reason an organisation exists — its purpose and values
questions.explanation: Pūtake is the reason an organisation exists. It shapes all financial decisions. For example, a food bank's pūtake is to ensure no one goes hungry — this drives every spending and saving decision they make.

activities.title: My Money Right Now
activities.prompt:
Think honestly about your own finances right now. You don't have to share this with anyone.

1. What are your main sources of income (however small)?
2. What do you spend most of your money on each week?
3. Do you think you're in surplus or deficit right now?
4. If you applied the 50/30/20 rule to your income, what would need to change?

Write 3–5 sentences. There are no wrong answers — this is just about being honest with yourself.
activities.nceaLink: This reflection builds self-awareness that supports AS92028 assessment — understanding your own financial situation before analysing an organisation's.
